planting.note: Surface sow (seeds require light to germinate) in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to moist soil in partial shade to full sun; do not cover seeds — press firmly to the surface and keep moist until germination in spring.

directions: Lobelia puberula is one of the most common and adaptable native lobelias of the southeastern coastal plain and piedmont, with densely pubescent (downy-hairy) stems and leaves and long racemes of clear blue, two-lipped flowers from August through October that are excellent hummingbird forage before the birds' fall migration south; it grows naturally in moist roadsides, woodland edges, fields, and streambanks and is one of the easiest native lobelias to establish in the moist, partially shaded border. More heat and humidity tolerant than L. cardinalis or L. siphilitica, it is the best blue lobelia for deep South gardens in zones 7–9. Surface sow seeds without covering — light is required for germination — in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil; seeds overwinter and germinate in spring.

planting.note: Surface sow in fall in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; southern lobelia naturally occurs in moist to wet bottomland forest understories and shaded stream margins and requires consistent moisture to thrive.

directions: Lobelia amoena is a native lobelia of moist, shaded lowland forests and swamp edges from North Carolina to Florida and west to Mississippi, producing tall racemes of bright blue to blue-violet flowers from August through October in habitats where many other flowering perennials cannot survive; it is less commonly cultivated than L. cardinalis or L. siphilitica but is increasingly appreciated as an outstanding shade-tolerant, moist-soil native for the lower South. The brilliant blue flowers on 3–4 foot stems are outstanding for hummingbirds and large bees; the plant gradually spreads by short rhizomes. Surface sow in fall in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade, keeping seeds on the soil surface as light aids germination.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown transplants in spring in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Georgia lobelia is restricted in the wild to pocosins, bay swamps, and wet pine flatwoods of the Southeast and requires consistently moist to wet soil in the garden — excellent in rain gardens and at pond margins.

directions: Lobelia georgiana is an uncommon native lobelia of the southeastern coastal plain from North Carolina to Georgia and northern Florida, found in the wild primarily in pocosins, wet pine savannas, and the moist margins of bay swamps; it produces tall racemes of vibrant, deep blue-violet flowers from August through October that are visually similar to L. siphilitica but adapted to the acidic, consistently wet soils of coastal plain flatwoods habitats. It is rarely available from nurseries but is a valuable ecological garden plant for moist, partially shaded situations in zones 7–9 where it supports late-season migrating hummingbirds. Plant nursery-grown transplants in spring in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic soil; do not allow to dry out at any point during the growing season.

planting.note: Surface sow in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; pale lobelia is an annual or biennial that self-seeds readily in suitable conditions, maintaining itself in the garden from year to year without replanting.

directions: Lobelia appendiculata is a delicate, small-flowered annual to biennial native lobelia of moist disturbed sites, roadsides, sandy flats, and open pine flatwoods in the southeastern coastal plain from Virginia to Texas; it produces wispy, branching stems with very narrow leaves and small pale-blue to white, two-lipped flowers with a distinctive appendage (hence appendiculata) on the calyx lobes, blooming from spring through summer. It is one of the smaller and more delicate native lobelias, useful as a naturalistic, self-seeding filler in moist native garden settings in the Deep South and Gulf Coast. Surface sow in fall without covering seeds; the plant self-seeds readily once established and will naturalize in appropriate moist, open conditions.

planting.note: Surface sow in fall in consistently moist to wet, very slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or organic soil in partial shade to full sun; Nuttall's lobelia occurs naturally in the wet, peaty soils of coastal plain bogs, pocosins, and wet pine savannas from New Jersey to Florida and requires consistently moist to wet conditions in garden settings.

directions: Lobelia nuttallii is a slender, delicate native lobelia of the Atlantic coastal plain, found in open, wet, acidic habitats including pitcher plant bogs, wet pine savannas, and seepage slopes; it produces very narrow, sparse leaves on slender stems and small, pale blue, typical lobelia-form flowers from June through September, blooming over a long period. While not showy by conventional standards, it is a charming and ecologically significant native plant for bog gardens, rain gardens, and other moist, partially shaded naturalistic plantings in zones 4–9; it self-seeds in appropriate wet conditions and gradually increases into colonies. Surface sow in fall without covering seeds — light aids germination — in consistently moist to wet, peaty or sandy acidic soil; do not allow to dry out.
